docs.text: CCDB ComputerCraftDB (CCDB) is a very lightweight SQL-like database management system. It's written 100% in Lua and designed to run within the ComputerCraft mod for Minecraft. Specs Language: Lua CLI + Bundler into 1 file Runs on PC with craftos-pc Runs on Minecraft with ComputerCraft mod Features Queries : SELECT , INSERT , UPDATE , DELETE Conditions : WHERE with operators < , > , <= , >= , != , = , % (like) Values : STRING , FLOAT , INTEGER , BOOLEAN , CALCULATED , NULL Not supported SCHEMA modification (CREATE, DROP, ALTER), made with a .json file JOINs (and foreign keys) Note on security This app is NOT designed to be secure. It is supposed to be used in a minecraft server, which is a trusted environment. For example, the string sanitation is very bad. It works... but it is not secure. Note If you see a "could not aquire lock" error, simply remove the path/to/your/db/table.json.lock file and try again. This is a known issue that happens when the server crashes while a lock is active, but it can also happen if the server is killed while a lock is active. Note 2 Due to json serialization issues, you cannot save more than ~700 entries in a table. I can't do anything about this, it's a limitation of ComputerCraft's json library. If you need to save more than 700 entries, you can split your data into multiple tables. !! 700 entries is for the example users table, which has 4 fields. If you have a table with more fields, the limit will be lower.
docs.title: Home

docs.text: Application usage Install CraftOS-PC Here Upload bin.lua and runstdin.lua Drag-and-drop bin.lua and runstdin.lua into craftos-pc Install the db server Run: bin.lua setup Run the db server Run: runstdin.lua Open a db In this doc, we will use dbtest, it's bundled with the default software Run in the CCDB term: OPEN(dbtest); Use the default user and pass (admin + adminpass) Then you can type your queries (see queries.md) Create a table You need to use the JSON-format (see table schema ) to create a table. Configuration (see config.md )
docs.title: App Usage

docs.text: App configuration Log config Location: config/log.json {"dbg": true/false, "log": true/false, "log_path": "path/to/logfile"} dbg : whether to print debug messages in the console log : whether to log queries to a file log_path : the path to the log file (relative to the current directory, absolute is recommended) Databases location config Location: cfg/dblocation.cfg /path/to/databases This path in the file must point to the directory where the databases are stored. Each database is a subdirectory in this directory. (and must have a ccdb.json file in it)
docs.title: Configuration

docs.text: Location: config/log.json {"dbg": true/false, "log": true/false, "log_path": "path/to/logfile"} dbg : whether to print debug messages in the console log : whether to log queries to a file log_path : the path to the log file (relative to the current directory, absolute is recommended)
docs.title: Log config

docs.text: Location: cfg/dblocation.cfg /path/to/databases This path in the file must point to the directory where the databases are stored. Each database is a subdirectory in this directory. (and must have a ccdb.json file in it)
docs.title: Databases location config

docs.text: Minecraft Computercraft integration This page describes how to use CCDB with Minecraft Computercraft. Apart from CraftOS-PC, you can also run CCDB on a real Minecraft server. You will need to upload the bin.lua files to your ComputerCraft computer, and then run them to start the database server. bin.lua setup Then, connect your client to the server in Minecraft with computercraft's "internet" API (rednet) Rednet module configuration Write the rednet module location (top, left, right, back, front) in the cfg/rednet_module file. Run the DB Run the bin.lua file to start the database server. bin.lua Client use See lua_api/client.md for the client API documentation.
docs.title: Minecraft-specific usage

docs.text: SQL Queries supported by CCDB INSERT (view queries ) INSERT INTO user (username, email) VALUES ('Alice22', 'alice22@example.com'); UPDATE (view update ) UPDATE user SET email = 'none@none.none' WHERE id = 1; DELETE (view delete ) DELETE FROM user WHERE id = 1; SELECT (view select ) SELECT age *2, username, 'I can , even do this!' FROM user WHERE age = age*2-30 OR username = 'admin'; WHERE CLAUSE (view where ) <query SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE> WHERE id = 1 AND (username = 'admin' OR username = 'Alice22'); Values To view informations about values (ex. literal, calcul, ...), view values.md Syntax EVERY query FINISHES WITH ; . You can't make two queries at once (this is not possible: query1; query2; ) Note The queries are case-sensitive . For example, SELECT is valid, but select is not. However, the column names and table names are not case-sensitive. For example, user and User refer to the same table.
docs.title: Queries

docs.text: INSERT INTO user (username, email) VALUES ('Alice22', 'alice22@example.com');
docs.title: INSERT (view queries)

docs.text: UPDATE user SET email = 'none@none.none' WHERE id = 1;
docs.title: UPDATE (view update)

docs.text: DELETE FROM user WHERE id = 1;
docs.title: DELETE (view delete)

docs.text: SELECT age *2, username, 'I can , even do this!' FROM user WHERE age = age*2-30 OR username = 'admin';
docs.title: SELECT (view select)

docs.text: <query SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE> WHERE id = 1 AND (username = 'admin' OR username = 'Alice22');
docs.title: WHERE CLAUSE (view where)

docs.text: To view informations about values (ex. literal, calcul, ...), view values.md
docs.title: Values

docs.text: EVERY query FINISHES WITH ; . You can't make two queries at once (this is not possible: query1; query2; )
docs.title: Syntax

docs.text: The queries are case-sensitive . For example, SELECT is valid, but select is not. However, the column names and table names are not case-sensitive. For example, user and User refer to the same table.
docs.title: Note

docs.text: Tools I provide a few devlopment tools to make your life easier when working with CCDB. Stress Tester Tests the performance of the server by sending a lot of queries at once. Located at stress_tester.lua Run STDIN This is used to quickly run some queries on the server without having to create a client. It reads queries from the standard input and prints the results to the console. Located at runstdin.lua "Compiler" This is NOT a compiler, but rather a tool to convert a directory of LUA files into an "installer" file that can be run on the server. This is useful so you can post ccdb pastebin (computercraft uses a lot pastebin to transfer files) and then run the installer to get all the files in the right place. It also adds a ".buildnumber" file to the output, which contains the build number of the installer. This is useful for versioning and debugging. (it's just the timestamp of the build) Located at compiler.lua .
docs.title: Dev tools

It also adds a ".buildnumber" file to the output, which contains the build number of the installer. This is useful for versioning and debugging. (it's just the timestamp of the build) Located at compiler.lua .

docs.text: Client API (lua) I provide a Lua API for the client side. ! Only works under Minecraft environment ! How to install Copy the client_lib.lua file to your project and require it in your code: local ccdb_client = require("client_lib") API Reference ccdb_client.set_up Description: Set up the rednet modem. Args: - modem : Modem side ("left", "right", "top", "bottom", "front", "back") Return: - nil Errors: - None ccdb_client.set_up("left") ccdb_client.safe_query Description: Sanitize a string to prevent SQL injection. (not really, just a basic implementation) Args: - str : The string to sanitize Return: - The sanitized string Errors: - None local safe_str = ccdb_client.safe_query("hello'; DROP TABLE users; --") ccdb_client.connect Description: Connect to a server (no db needed). Make sure the server is OK by sending a ping/pong message. Args: - host : The host to connect to (rednet host) Return: - nil Errors: - Host not found - No response from server (timeout: 5s) - Invalid response from server (not pong) ccdb_client.connect(host) -- I don't remember how the host looks like, check the rednet documentation ccdb_client.login_db Description: Log in to a database. Make sure the database exists and the password/username is correct. Args: - db : The name of the database to log in to - username : The username to log in with - password : The password to log in with Return: - nil Errors: - No response from server (timeout: 5s) - Invalid response from server (not success or error), will print the response ccdb_client.login_db("dbtest", "admin", "adminpass") ccdb_client.query Description: Send a query to the server. Make sure the query is valid and the server is ready to receive queries. Args: - query : The query to send (string) Return: - The response from the server (object) Errors: - No response from server (timeout: 10s) - Invalid response from server, won't print the response local response = ccdb_client.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 1")
docs.title: Client

docs.text: I provide a Lua API for the client side. ! Only works under Minecraft environment !

docs.text: Server API This document describes the API for the server side of CCDB. This is used when you don't want to create a server, but rather want to use the server functionality in your own code, for example caching some db datas into the client (like with SQLite in a real-world scenario) How to install Same as the server bin setup Usage To use the server API, you need to require the src/lib_ccdb.lua file in your code: local runlib = require("src/lib_ccdb") API Reference runlib.opendb Description: Open a database. Args: - name : The name of the database to open Returns: - db : The opened database object, or nil if the database could not be opened Errors: - None (except for internal db errors) Returns: - nil runlib.opendb("dbtest") runlib.run Description: Run a query on the database. Args: - query : The query to run, as a string Returns: - result : The result of the query, as a table - nil : If the query failed, nil is returned, and an error message is printed to the console Errors: - None (except for internal db errors). If the query is invalid, an error message is printed to the console. local result = runlib.run("SELECT * FROM users") runlib.safe_val Description: Sanitize a value for use in a query. This is used to prevent SQL injection attacks. Args: - val : The value to convert Returns: - safe_val : The sanitized value, as a string Errors: - None local safe_val = runlib.safe_val("O'Reilly") -- safe_val will be "OReilly"
docs.title: Server

If the query is invalid, an error message is printed to the console. local result = runlib.run("SELECT * FROM users")
docs.title: runlib.run

docs.text: Description: Sanitize a value for use in a query. This is used to prevent SQL injection attacks. Args: - val : The value to convert Returns: - safe_val : The sanitized value, as a string Errors: - None local safe_val = runlib.safe_val("O'Reilly") -- safe_val will be "OReilly"

docs.text: Delete query The delete query works like this: DELETE FROM <table-name> WHERE <condition>; Special cases You can delete all the rows of a table by omitting the WHERE clause: DELETE FROM <table-name>; (view where for conditions) Example DELETE FROM user WHERE id = 1; DELETE FROM user;
docs.title: Delete

docs.text: The delete query works like this: DELETE FROM <table-name> WHERE <condition>;
docs.title: Delete query

docs.text: You can delete all the rows of a table by omitting the WHERE clause: DELETE FROM <table-name>; (view where for conditions)
docs.title: Special cases

docs.text: DELETE FROM user WHERE id = 1; DELETE FROM user;
docs.title: Example

docs.text: Insert query The insert query works like this: INSERT INTO <table-name> (col1, col2) VALUES (val1, val2); col1, col2 : Special cases Calc values aren't supported in insert queries, so you can't do something like this: INSERT INTO <table-name> (col1, col2) VALUES (val1, val2 + 1); (see values.md for values) Example INSERT INTO user (username, email) VALUES ('Alice22', 'alice22@example.com');
docs.title: Insert

docs.text: The insert query works like this: INSERT INTO <table-name> (col1, col2) VALUES (val1, val2); col1, col2 :
docs.title: Insert query

docs.text: Calc values aren't supported in insert queries, so you can't do something like this: INSERT INTO <table-name> (col1, col2) VALUES (val1, val2 + 1); (see values.md for values)
docs.title: Special cases

docs.text: INSERT INTO user (username, email) VALUES ('Alice22', 'alice22@example.com');
docs.title: Example

docs.text: Select query The select query works like this: SELECT <col-name>, <other-col-name> FROM <table-name> WHERE <condition> ORDER BY <col-name> ASC|DESC LIMIT <limit-nbr>; SELECT <col/other-col-name> : The name of the columns (ex. col1, col2 ) <table-name> : The name of the table <condition> : See where.md ORDER BY <col-name> ASC|DESC : Sort by col. You can use multiple cols (ex. ORDER BY col1 ASC, col2 DESC ) LIMIT <limit-nbr> : Limit to X objects (ex. LIMIT 1 ) Examples SELECT name, age FROM users WHERE age > 18 ORDER BY age DESC LIMIT 10; This will select the name and age of users where the age is greater than 18, order them by age in descending order, and limit the results to 10 users.
docs.title: Select

docs.text: The select query works like this: SELECT <col-name>, <other-col-name> FROM <table-name> WHERE <condition> ORDER BY <col-name> ASC|DESC LIMIT <limit-nbr>; SELECT <col/other-col-name> : The name of the columns (ex. col1, col2 ) <table-name> : The name of the table <condition> : See where.md ORDER BY <col-name> ASC|DESC : Sort by col. You can use multiple cols (ex. ORDER BY col1 ASC, col2 DESC ) LIMIT <limit-nbr> : Limit to X objects (ex. LIMIT 1 )
docs.title: Select query

docs.text: SELECT name, age FROM users WHERE age > 18 ORDER BY age DESC LIMIT 10; This will select the name and age of users where the age is greater than 18, order them by age in descending order, and limit the results to 10 users.
docs.title: Examples

docs.text: Update query The update query works like this: UPDATE <table-name> SET <col-name> = <value>, <other-col-name> = <other-value> WHERE <condition>; UPDATE <table-name> : The name of the table SET <col-name> = <value>, <other-col-name> = <other-value> : The name of the columns and the new values (ex. col1 = 'new value', col2 = 42 ) <condition> : See where.md Special case: update with no condition The update query can also be used without a condition. In this case, all the rows of the table will be updated with the new values. For example: UPDATE users SET age = age + 1; Note: The update values can use calculations. For example: UPDATE user SET age = 0 WHERE age = NULL;
docs.title: Update

docs.text: The update query works like this: UPDATE <table-name> SET <col-name> = <value>, <other-col-name> = <other-value> WHERE <condition>; UPDATE <table-name> : The name of the table SET <col-name> = <value>, <other-col-name> = <other-value> : The name of the columns and the new values (ex. col1 = 'new value', col2 = 42 ) <condition> : See where.md
docs.title: Update query

docs.text: The update query can also be used without a condition. In this case, all the rows of the table will be updated with the new values. For example: UPDATE users SET age = age + 1;
docs.title: Special case: update with no condition

docs.text: UPDATE user SET age = 0 WHERE age = NULL;
docs.title: Note: The update values can use calculations. For example:

docs.text: CCDB Values Literals STRING The string literal is a string value. (Enclosed in single quotes '...'). THE STRING SHOULDN'T CONTAIN SINGLE QUOTES. You can't store a string with single quotes in it. If you need to store a string with single quotes, you'll need to replace single quotes with a different character, such as a double quote... Use safe_val to sanitize the string before using it in a query. (see server API and client API for more information on safe_val) FLOAT The float value is a number with a decimal point. (Ex. 3.9 ) INTEGER The integer value is a whole number. (Ex. 39 ) BOOLEAN The boolean value can be: TRUE or FALSE (needs to be in uppercase) CALCULATED A calculated value is a value that is calculated from other values. For example, age + 1 is a calculated value that adds 1 to the age value. NULL The null value represents the column value that is not defined. Writen as NULL (needs to be in uppercase).
docs.title: Values

docs.text: The string literal is a string value. (Enclosed in single quotes '...'). THE STRING SHOULDN'T CONTAIN SINGLE QUOTES. You can't store a string with single quotes in it. If you need to store a string with single quotes, you'll need to replace single quotes with a different character, such as a double quote... Use safe_val to sanitize the string before using it in a query. (see server API and client API for more information on safe_val)
docs.title: STRING

docs.text: The float value is a number with a decimal point. (Ex. 3.9 )
docs.title: FLOAT

docs.text: The integer value is a whole number. (Ex. 39 )
docs.title: INTEGER

docs.text: The boolean value can be: TRUE or FALSE (needs to be in uppercase)
docs.title: BOOLEAN

docs.text: A calculated value is a value that is calculated from other values. For example, age + 1 is a calculated value that adds 1 to the age value.
docs.title: CALCULATED

docs.text: The null value represents the column value that is not defined. Writen as NULL (needs to be in uppercase).
docs.title: NULL

docs.text: Where conditions Conditions are used in the WHERE clause of a query. They allow you to filter the results based on specific criteria. Supported conditions < (less than) > (greater than) <= (less than or equal to) >= (greater than or equal to) != (not equal to) = (equal to) % (like) The like operator allows you to search for a specified pattern in a column. You can use the % wildcard to represent any sequence of characters. For example, name % 'John%' will match any name that starts with "John". And you can use _ to represent a single character. For example, name % 'J_n%' will match any name that starts with "J", followed by any single character, and then "n". Examples SELECT name FROM users WHERE age > 18; This will select the name of users where the age is greater than 18. SELECT name FROM users WHERE name % 'John%'; This will select the name of users where the name starts with "John". Concatenation of conditions You can concatenate conditions using AND and OR . For example: SELECT name FROM users WHERE age > 18 AND name % 'John%' OR name = 'admin'; AND has a higher priority than OR. (First: age > 18 AND name % 'John%' , then OR name = 'admin' )
docs.title: Where conditions

docs.text: Conditions are used in the WHERE clause of a query. They allow you to filter the results based on specific criteria.
docs.title: Where conditions

docs.text: The like operator allows you to search for a specified pattern in a column. You can use the % wildcard to represent any sequence of characters. For example, name % 'John%' will match any name that starts with "John". And you can use _ to represent a single character. For example, name % 'J_n%' will match any name that starts with "J", followed by any single character, and then "n".
docs.title: % (like)

docs.text: SELECT name FROM users WHERE age > 18; This will select the name of users where the age is greater than 18. SELECT name FROM users WHERE name % 'John%'; This will select the name of users where the name starts with "John".
docs.title: Examples

docs.text: You can concatenate conditions using AND and OR . For example: SELECT name FROM users WHERE age > 18 AND name % 'John%' OR name = 'admin'; AND has a higher priority than OR. (First: age > 18 AND name % 'John%' , then OR name = 'admin' )
docs.title: Concatenation of conditions

docs.text: Database creation The json schema for database creation is this: { "tables": { "table1": "table1.json" }, "login": { "username": "password" } } Explanation tables : Here, we register the tables of the database. (name-of-table: "name-of-table.json"). For the table schema, see table.md login : Here, we register the login credentials for the database. The username is the key and the password is the value. (ex. "admin": "admin123" )
docs.title: Database

docs.text: { "tables": { "table1": "table1.json" }, "login": { "username": "password" } }
docs.title: The json schema for database creation is this:

docs.text: tables : Here, we register the tables of the database. (name-of-table: "name-of-table.json"). For the table schema, see table.md login : Here, we register the login credentials for the database. The username is the key and the password is the value. (ex. "admin": "admin123" )
docs.title: Explanation

docs.text: Table schema The json schema for a table is this: { "schema": { "current_sysid": 1, "name": "item", "indexes": [ { "field": "id", "type": "pk" }, { "field": "item_id", "type": "unique" }, { "field": "id", "type": "ai", "start": 1, "current": 1 } ], "fields": { "id": { "type": ["integer"], "nullable": false, "default": null }, "item_id": { "type": ["varchar", 50], "nullable": false, "default": null }, "item_count": { "type": ["integer"], "nullable": true, "default": 0 } } }, "data": [ { "?sysid?": 1, "id": 1, "item_id": "minecraft:stone", "item_count": 64 }, { "?sysid?": 2, "id": 2, "item_id": "minecraft:dirt", "item_count": 32 } ] } Explanation schema : The structure of the table current_sysid : The current system id, used for internal purposes (auto-incrementing) name : The name of the table (unused, left because idk why) indexes : The indexes of the table, an array of objects fields : The fields of the table, an object data : The data of the table, an array of objects (each object is a row) Indexes Primary key (pk) The primary key index is used to uniquely identify each row in the table. It is defined by setting the type to pk and specifying the field that will be used as the primary key. Example: { "field": "id", "type": "pk" } Unique key (unique) The unique key index is used to ensure that all values in a column are different from each other. It is defined by setting the type to unique and specifying the field that will be used as the unique key. Example: { "field": "item_id", "type": "unique" } Auto-incrementing key (ai) The AI key index is used to automatically generate a unique value for each new row inserted into the table. It is defined by setting the type to ai . The field specifies the column that will be auto-incremented, start specifies the starting value, and current keeps track of the current value. Example: { "field": "id", "type": "ai", "start": 1, "current": 1 } Fields Each field in the fields object represents a column in the table. key: The name of the column type: The data type of the column (e.g., integer, varchar) in an array. The first element is the type name, and the second element (optional) can specify additional parameters (e.g., maximum length for varchar). Supported int : A whole number (e.g., 1, 2, 3) varchar : A string of characters (e.g., "hello", "world"). The maximum length can be specified as a second element in the type array (e.g., ["varchar", 50] for a maximum length of 50 characters). float : A number with a decimal point (e.g., 3.14, 2.718) boolean : A true or false value (e.g., true, false) null : Do you really want to use it? text : A varchar, but with no maximum length (e.g., "This is a long text that can be of any length.") Nullable The nullable property allows the value of the field to be null. Default The default property specifies the default value for the field if no value is provided during insertion. Data ?sysid? : This is an internal field used for auto-incrementing purposes. It should not be included in the data when inserting new rows, as it will be automatically generated by the system. Each object in the data array represents a row in the table, with key-value pairs corresponding to the column names and their respective values.
docs.title: Tables

docs.text: { "schema": { "current_sysid": 1, "name": "item", "indexes": [ { "field": "id", "type": "pk" }, { "field": "item_id", "type": "unique" }, { "field": "id", "type": "ai", "start": 1, "current": 1 } ], "fields": { "id": { "type": ["integer"], "nullable": false, "default": null }, "item_id": { "type": ["varchar", 50], "nullable": false, "default": null }, "item_count": { "type": ["integer"], "nullable": true, "default": 0 } } }, "data": [ { "?sysid?": 1, "id": 1, "item_id": "minecraft:stone", "item_count": 64 }, { "?sysid?": 2, "id": 2, "item_id": "minecraft:dirt", "item_count": 32 } ] }
docs.title: The json schema for a table is this:

docs.text: schema : The structure of the table current_sysid : The current system id, used for internal purposes (auto-incrementing) name : The name of the table (unused, left because idk why) indexes : The indexes of the table, an array of objects fields : The fields of the table, an object data : The data of the table, an array of objects (each object is a row)
